title: The color orange was named after the fruit — not the other way around.
short: Before oranges arrived in Europe, the color was called 'geoluhread' (yellow-red) in English. The fruit's name came from Sanskrit 'nāranga,' and eventually the color borrowed the fruit's name.
twist: For centuries, humans saw orange but had no word for it. Just vibes.
more: The word 'orange' entered English in the 14th century from Old French 'orenge,' which came from Arabic 'nāranj,' from Sanskrit 'nāranga.' Before this, English speakers described the color as 'red-yellow' or 'saffron.' This means there was a period in history where people could see orange but had to describe it as a blend of other colors. Language literally shaped how people categorized their visual experience.

title: It rains diamonds on Neptune and Uranus.
short: Extreme pressure and temperature deep inside these ice giants compress carbon atoms into solid diamond. These diamond chunks then 'rain' down through the atmosphere toward the core.
twist: Somewhere right now, it's raining diamonds. Just not here.
more: Scientists at SLAC National Accelerator Laboratory confirmed this in 2017 by recreating the conditions inside Neptune — using high-powered lasers to compress hydrocarbons. They watched diamond crystals form in real time. The diamonds inside Neptune and Uranus could be millions of carats in size, slowly sinking through liquid hydrogen toward a solid diamond core. The total mass of diamond inside these planets could be staggering.

title: There's a fish that can walk, climb trees, and survive out of water for days.
short: The climbing perch (Anabas testudineus) uses its gill plates as legs, breathes atmospheric air, and can travel across land for days searching for new bodies of water.
twist: Evolution: 'Stay in the water.' This fish: 'No.'
more: The climbing perch has a special labyrinth organ that lets it breathe air directly. When its pond dries up, it 'walks' overland using spiny gill covers as legs, sometimes covering several hundred meters. It can survive out of water for up to 6 days if kept moist. Reports from the 1800s describe them being found in trees, though scientists debate whether they actually climb or are deposited there by floods.

title: Your body generates enough electricity to power a small lightbulb.
short: The human body produces about 100 watts of power at rest — mostly as heat, but a portion is genuine electrical energy flowing through your nervous system at up to 120 meters per second.
twist: You are a battery that worries about emails.
more: Nerve impulses travel as electrical signals generated by ion channels in cell membranes. Each neuron fires at about 0.07 volts — tiny individually, but with 86 billion neurons firing millions of times per second, it adds up. Your heart alone generates enough electricity to be detected by sensors several feet away. The electrical activity of your brain is what EEG machines measure — proof that you are, literally, an electric being.
